Alright, now we're going to go to step six, which is testimonials. The testimonials are the fundamental proof and support for your big promise and your credibility. So your big promise is the promise you're making to your reader, your prospect. You're saying, if you come along with me, you will get X, or this will happen to you, or you'll be able to accomplish this. And the credibility is, why should he believe you? Why should anyone believe you? And one of the, going back to the bar room scene where you're sitting there and someone, you're sitting there innocently and someone comes up and says to the bartender, give me a double, I've had a horrible day and the bartender says, Bob, what's wrong? Bob says, oh man, and what he goes on to explain is exactly the situation that what you have helps or solves or mitigates. So then you turn to him and you say, and you introduce yourself and you start talking. However, this is still an inherently hostile situation. He is looking for a reason to bolt. He is not necessarily happy to have found you, even if his problem is dire. He always has one foot ready to go if you say the wrong thing, if you seem like you're really pushing some kind of sales thing on them. There's a lot of reasons that people don't want to talk to somebody, even if it seems they know a lot about what the situation is that they're in or how to solve it if they've been there before. So the testimonial is kind of like while you're sitting there, someone walks by that knows Bob, and says, Bob, this is John Carleton, don't you know who John is? And then Bob says, no, who is he? He said, oh, he helped me a lot. I went through his simple writing system and he taught me how to write ads and it exploded my business and everything was wonderful. So for you sitting there, somebody comes by and says, oh my God, Bob, this is Susie Q. Susie Q is one of the experts in BLAA and helped me and helped other people I knew and it's just amazing. I can't believe you're talking to her. You should really listen to what she says. That's what we call a third party endorsement. So if you say something, if you say I'm the hottest thing since sliced bread, I am great, I've never lose and I've done everything right all my life. If you say it, it's bragging. If someone else comes along and says, oh my God, this person here is better than sliced bread, this person has done wonderful things, then it's more of an endorsement. It's a third party endorsement. Even better if the person that endorses you actually is known or knows the person you're trying to convince. So that's why sometimes celebrities come into there or people that hold some credibility already for the prospect. It would be borrowed credibility. If someone they respect respects you, then that respect can be translated. So testimonials are partly that, partly establishing the credibility, but they're also just laying out what happens to people they get involved with you. People talk about this, but you want as many testimonials as possible. You may not use them all, but they will all come in handy. And getting testimonials is a good way to keep on top of how you're doing out there. Because if people are complaining and they're not giving you any testimonials, then you're doing something wrong. So the way to get into testimonials, and I'm going to have a number of examples for you to look at, they should be specific, short, and spicy. So when we get into specifics, it's like nothing rambling. They should not be long. They should be as short as possible. Although some long ones, I'm going to show you some long ones that are pretty damn good. But the spiciness is what matters. It's not a boring, hey, this person's really great, but it's very specific. This person helped me do this. And here's how my life changed because of it. And the spiciness comes from the little mini stories that we get into. So here's some tips. Get them yourself. When you're out and about and somebody comes up and says, hey, Bob, wow, that book you wrote or that advice you gave me or that product that I bought from you really was really great, then your response should be, dude, you owe me a testimonial. Most people who are happy enough to tell you they're happy are happy enough to also be willing to put their name on a testimonial that you can then use in video and written in all kinds of advertising venues. So get in the habit of asking them if you can have a testimonial that they will sign. The really easy way to do this is a short before and after story. Before I met Bob, I was blank. I was broke. I could never get a date. I was a failed architect, whatever. And then the after. After I took Bob's advice or I bought Bob's book or I got involved in Bob's program or whatever or spent 15 minutes consulting with Bob, then all of this great stuff happened. I got married. My life is a lot better. I'm making money, all of this stuff. Before and after stories, really tight little ones. And you'll see some examples when we get to that. It's really important that you have a point. And this is where we get back to the features and benefits. Back up specific features and benefits with testimonials. Sometimes you will get five testimonials from people all saying more or less the same thing. Product was great, really happy, liked it a lot, you know, thumbs up. And it's not specific. Somebody reading it has no idea why the person likes it. The person could be your brother-in-law or your best friend. So they don't know what it is that's going on. So if instead of saying, yeah, thumbs up, it was a great product, they say, this product helped me do X and I got this result from it. Or I'm so happy because now I know how to get better gas mileage. Or I now know how to write an ad whenever I need to do it. So when you have your list of features and benefits, backing those up with testimonials. If somebody comes and says, wow, I really liked your product, you can say, what was it about the product that you liked? Or how did it help you? What specific part of your life or your business or whatever you're involved with got better and how from what I helped? And they will usually say, oh, it was that part about, you know, never doing, you know, always doing a primer coat before you paint the car. Or, you know, the tip you gave me on X or the way you helped me do blah. So have a point. Don't just be vague. Yeah, he's great. Say he's great because he helped me do this and I got these results. It's perfectly fair to take somebody who has said, I'd love to give you a testimonial. Or who said, this is a great product or service and I highly endorse it. It's perfectly okay to go back and work with them and say, you know, what was it about your experience that helped it out? Help them verbalize it. Help them tell their before and after story. Ask them, what was it like before you encountered the product or service and what happened after that made you so happy that you wanted to give a testimonial? It's not just okay. It's really how a lot of testimonials get worked out. Again, if you say it, it's bragging. Someone else says it, it's third party validation. This can't be overemphasized. People don't like braggarts. They don't like people who say unsubstantiated things about themselves. It's generally a negative. If someone else says it though, it's just, wow, this guy must have something going on if somebody else will say that about it. So keep in mind that bar scene with a fan walking by with real language including slang. How would somebody actually say this? A lot of people in business take testimonials and they clean them up. We use a lot of video testimonials and, you know, we'll do one take testimonials with just the camera on our cell phone and use that with the ums, the ahs, the stuttering, all of that. That's how people talk. That is how they actually communicate something that's important to them. So there's nothing wrong with that. Really, really slick rehearsed testimonials or rehearsed anything really is kind of seen as too slick, suspect, and you're not sure it's real. So the more real it is, like someone just walking by and go, wow, I can't believe you're talking to blah, blah, blah. And I have testimonials from people who say, wow, can't believe what happened to me after I read your book or something like that. Some more tips. Never make them up. If necessary, if you don't have any testimonials because it's a new product or service, then this is part of your daily homework as you go about your career or your business. Get your product into people's hands or perform your service for people and get feedback. Ask them, how was it? Did you like it? Was it good? You know, how did it help you? What was going on? And there's perfectly okay to go out and solicit testimonials. You just say, you know, I'd love to get your feedback. And if it's good, then you can say, do you mind if I use that and have your picture and signature on it? A lot of times people are so happy that you've helped them with a problem, they will say yes. Do not make them up. That is a sure sign that your marketing is going straight down the tubes. Visuals matter. Mix in video when possible to increase credibility. Use text when you're using photos. So we have banks of testimonials that are all video. If we do use video, we usually have a headline, we will pull out one of the main benefits that the person talks about. And sometimes we'll explain other things underneath. So there's like maybe a little mini headline and a little caption underneath. But the short, pithy videos carry the weight. Sometimes we'll mix them in with text testimonials and we'll usually like to use a photo. There's a photo of the person with the quote of their testimonial and then their name. You'll see examples of this in just a second. This is all about credibility too. Just having to line this says, Susie Q from upland Ohio says, this is a great product, five stars. You don't know if Susie Q is real. You don't know what she looks like. You don't know if she's a middle-aged woman from the Midwest or a Moroccan consulate assistant. You don't know anything about them and you can't fit it into your head of why this is important as you're reading this or as you're looking at it. So the more credibility you can give to the person by using their name, having a picture, having some context if they're in a video. This all helps. A mix of celebrities and nobody's is best. Having all celebrities give you your testimonials, that's kind of suspect too because it's kind of like log rolling. Log rolling in the publishing industry is if you'll say something nice about my novel, then I'll say something nice about your novel. And so people are trading off little testimonials or blurbs all the time. So you can't be sure if the major author really even read the book of this guy, but he's trading it off because he's going to get a quote from this guy for his next book. And so having all celebrities can be suspect, but having a few celebrities and have celebrities say something real and be honest about it is very, very good. But nobody's, people who you don't know from Adam who come in, those can be some of your best testimonials because these are real people. They are essentially saying, I'm just like you. I was in the same situation you were in. I had my doubts. I got into this. I took Bob's course or I bought Bob's book or I had Bob's product and everything changed and it was great and I urge you to do it too. Interview subjects to find the before and after story and the details. So when people say, I made a lot of money, how much money was made? What exact results were gained? A lot of times people don't want to give away state secrets. So they don't want to say exactly how much money they made in a business transaction or how much more lucrative their career has gotten since they've learned these secrets or whatever. But sometimes they do. Sometimes they're quite open about this. We quadrupled profits is good. That's not very exact. But sometimes people say we quadrupled profits. We were only making $100,000 a year. Now we're making $400,000 a year. That's how good this stuff is. So keep pressing for details. Listen for accidental testimonials too. Get permission and use them. If you're walking down the hallway at a seminar or something and somebody just casually says, yeah, I used, hey, there's Bob Smith. I've used his product before and it's pretty good. If you hear that or you meet somebody just comes up and meets you and just says, yeah, I've used your product for years. It's pretty good. They're just doing that. They're just talking. Get permission, ask them if you can use the testimonial and then go ahead and interview them a little bit. Most people, maybe not most, yeah, in many cases, most people who have benefited from what you sell or what you offer people are quite willing to pay back a little bit. And testimonials are an easy way for them to help spread the word. They're helping to put your product into other people's hands who are just as deserving of better results as they were. And again, short sizzling pithy and to the point, also real and not overproduced. I know there are some marketers out there who really overproduced their testimonials so they look like little Hollywood movies. I think that's a huge mistake because it affects the credibility. Real conversations from real people is what gets your point across. So here's some examples. Let me just read them out. Frank Kern, this would be an example. This is how it actually appeared in the advertising that we were using. So there was a little headline here over $10 million in one day. There's a picture of Frank. I don't know how many of you are familiar with Frank, but he's a very, very successful marketer in the online world. So he said, just before the famous Stop and At launch, everything was in doubt. We cornered John for advice. And in just a few minutes, he provided such clarity that the fog melted away and I was finally able to write the pitch that brought in over $10 million in one day. Now that's a good enough testimony right there. It's specific. It tells a little story. It sets it up. Everything was in doubt. This is kind of a before and after. However, he went on. It's almost like the one-two punch. That's twice he's helped me. Long ago, when I was broke, clueless, and desperate, I somehow found John's kick-ass course and halfway through, a calm confidence came over me. I told my wife that everything was going to be all right because this really made sense. When John talks about changing your life, he's not kidding around. So this is a celebrity giving a very personal, very specific testimonial. Here's another expert, Jim Edwards. And he is well-known as an expert in the market that this came out in. So he was a celebrity. If you want to make money with sales copy, anything John Carleton's ever written needs to be right at your fingertips. The money I've made just from the bullets I've been able to write as a result of modeling and learning from John easily gets into the high six figures. So he's not being super specific, but he's giving enough information to tell you it was a lot of money. There's a reason people rip off John's copy. It's the best in the business. If I had to start all over again online today, I'd only need three things to get back to my current level. One, John's copyrighting knowledge. Two, an HTML editor. Three, a website. With these simple tools, I could conquer any part of the Internet I set my mind to. John's stuff is that good. That's a little story. These are not short. These are examples of little longer ones, but there's nothing I would take out of this. This is a guy who's bearing his soul. He's given me a lot of kudos. He's being specific about it. The great Gary Halbert, who I worked with for many, many years, is still well known. Even years after he's passed on, is well known as one of the go-to guys in marketing. His stuff is very good. His sons have continued his legacy, and his stuff is still good today. And this was a great quote he gave me. John is the first guy I call when I have a marketing problem. His insights and marketing prowess are unparalleled among the working advertising experts I know, and I know them all. The money available when you learn what insiders like John has to share is just insane. In my book, there is John, and no one else is even close. So this is like a celebrity. He's using some slang. It's just insane, the money available. And it's a little shorter. It's a little to the point. It was a very, very valuable testimony I got. Here's another expert example. Seven-figure money machine was the name of his product, Ken McCarthy. He said, I heard John in a seminar say something about writing headlines. I'd never heard or read anyone else say before. And believe me, I've been a serious and successful student of copyright for over 10 years. The result, I came up with a headline that crashed through all the noise in a very, very crowded marketplace. And starting from zero, rapidly created a new seven-figure, $1 million plus, a year money machine that's only just getting warmed up. So he's kind of specific. He's sharing the kind of before and after, starting from zero, and he's talking about an epiphany that he had. So even more tips. Capturing real voices is critical. Again, being too slick, being too Hollywood eyes, that's very suspect. The video, get the point straight before turning on the camera so there's no mumbling, but do accept small mistakes. So just go over what you're going to say. The main points would be something like, be sure you say what happened, how bad things were before, and then be sure that you mention how good things are now and be specific like we discussed. Find the subhead hook, the essence of the testimonial. That would be the little mini headline that would be above it. We'll have some more examples of that in a second. Again, there's no need to spill state secrets, rounded off estimates and percentages as opposed to exact amounts are fine. But you're still letting people know what the, it wasn't just, yeah, that was really good. I've had people give me testimonials like that. Your stuff's really good. Well, how is it good? How can we get more specific? Precise numbers when available are priceless. And as you've seen, sometimes people are quite willing to give out precise numbers. Answer the fundamental question, what happened? I think Frank Kearns was one of the best. He talked about they were in turmoil and then I gave him some advice and everything kind of cleared up and they went off and they had one of the biggest days ever. Here's some more examples. Four million dollars and a complete life change. That would be the little headline pulled out of this. John, ever since we met a few years ago, my life has completely changed. I'd say the amount of money my company has made directly from what you've taught me is in the neighborhood of four million dollars, maybe more. What can I say other than thanks a million and I owe you big time? We don't have a picture with that one. John Reese. John Carlton is one of the best marketers in marketing history. I have learned a ton from him and you should soak up everything from him that you can. It will only help you make more money from your internet marketing efforts. So that's kind of a non-specific celebrity testimonial. You want a few of those out there. It was really great that John said this about me. Nothing specific but it still carried a lot of weight. You don't want a lot of testimonials like that though. You want to get down to ones like this, which is Robert Pierce, created millions in profit. John has created millions in profit for us. We pitted his ads and letters against big city ad agencies, PR firms and writers with lots of awards, dot, dot, dot. And John slaughtered them all. He consistently hits home runs for us. A 20 to 1 return in profit is not unusual. He has saved our butts on several occasions. That phrase, he saved our butts on several occasions, that's real talk. That's not a slick edited piece. This is what he actually said when we got it down. This is a great one by Lisa Wagner. Tripled response. You know that new hook you insisted I try my sales pitch? It tripled response. This translates into several hundred thousand dollars in sales over the coming year. You are on my Christmas gift list for life, dude. That's a great little IGF. I mentioned that and I thought this goes into the testimonial. And a bunch of others. I love this one by John LaTourette. Worked so good it was scary. Just finished a 1000 trial mailing with new copy using Carlton's concepts. Raked in $22,000 the first week. Worked so good it was scary. Those kind of lines are gold. They help you position in the reader's mind or in the listener's mind what it meant to someone else. And people really identify with that. As a last one, one tip, one new car. On your advice I edited my copy using your action verbs. You're getting that free report by the way. This killer strategy alone allowed me to buy a new car for cash after the first campaign. So that's being very specific. Was able to buy a new car. We all know what new cars cost and that's a very good example. So that's it for testimonials. Very, very important. And I think we'll go to the Q&A now. Okay. Testimonials. Thousands and thousands of questions, examples of testimonials. So I'm going to pull out the ones that I, both from a lot of students asking these questions and from real-world business experience, there's a couple of these I know are important. And I think augment some of what John just gave you as really solid examples. One that if people start knowing a little bit about advertising or they start searching around looking for information testimonials, they're going to see a lot of legal stuff. So while we're not here to give anybody legal advice, we are not lawyers, we are not pretending to be lawyers, etc., etc., when maybe should you have a lawyer look over your testimonials in what markets or what spaces? Yeah, I think in heavily regulated markets you should get legal advice from an attorney just anyway because when you get into markets like weight loss, which is heavily regulated and there are things you can say and can't say where the feds will come down on your butt very, very quickly. And it's that way because it's had such a rocky history with bad players in the market. The financial industry, if you're selling stocks sometimes, that's a heavily regulated market. You can't just come out and start making stuff up or saying things that don't count out to the way things are done in these regulated markets. They're regulated for a reason. And there was a third market, I think we talked about, anything that has ingestibles and that means supplements, anything pills, mixes, anything that you put together to drink down, those are also heavily regulated because you're getting involved with the FDA who rightly so wants to regulate what Americans buy and put into their body. This goes back to the old snake oil days of the late 1890s where there were a lot of traveling circus shows who had a medicine man who would have a big barrel full of whiskey and he'd put rattlesnake heads into it and claim that it cured cancer and dandruff and sexual problems and everything. And of course people got sick and died and everything so they started regulating even back way before there were federal agencies. And so there are heavily regulated markets that you just plain need legal advice no matter what you do. As far as testimonials here, find out what the, if you don't know what the competition says and is able to say and is not able to say or can't say without getting in trouble, then you need to do more market research. You need to find out more about it. There are a lot of markets out there that are full of unethical social paths who will say and do anything to make a sale. In fact, there are some markets like the diet and weight loss market where sometimes people just put into their business plan, they're going to do time in jail because they're just willing to do that because there's so much money to be made. I'm assuming you've got an ethical product and you want to be known as an ethical businessman so you want to play by the rules. When you find out there are rules in your industry, follow them. Find out what the regulations are and do that. I'm not a lawyer, as Stan said. We don't give legal advice. There are times when you just have to run stuff by a lawyer. Yeah, I would say, and just to augment that, for the vast majority of markets and ads you're ever going to run across, you might create, if the person said this, it's okay to put in your ad. However, in specific markets, that is not true. Financial services come just, you very may very well have done A, B, and C and that person now made a lot of money. However, that does not mean you can use that in a testimony. These are rare cases. You should know enough about your business and your market if that's the case. Another point that, can you pay people for testimonials? You don't have to tell them the truth. By the word pay, it's like if I give you $10, will you give me a testimonial? That is a kind of unethical and B, if you do it, you actually could do it, but you have to disclose that you've done that. Now, some people say, I will give you my product for free and in exchange for that, I want a testimonial of how you can say whatever you want, but I want to get that. That is actually still compensating them for giving you a testimonial. You have to say somewhere that you gave them a free book or a free example of the thing or a free consultation or whatever. That has to be disclosed. There you go. Talk to your lawyer about that. Yeah. Yeah. I would say that last example is a common one for almost every business at some point in time, but you just nailed it. That is not illegal, however, it is a form of compensation. Just how you present your testimonials. If you gave someone the product, you didn't ask them to lie in the testimony, but you gave it to them, you're better off making that known in the testimonial. It doesn't mean they didn't really like it, didn't really help them. It didn't really provide the benefits you say. It could be as simple as, Bob, thanks for giving me that product and giving me a chance to try it out. I think people would be foolish not to give this a try and get the results that I got, which were blah, blah. Right. And then from there on, everything else, what makes a good one is what makes a good one. Okay. A couple of different questions that are broadly about the same thing. This is something that is pretty common, let's say in my business, our business, and I see customers come to us all the time with this. You have a really happy customer out there who's busy, so they come back to you with something like, hey, you asked for testimonial, you asked for an evaluation, and you're clear why you want it, and their response is something along the lines of, you know, I'm really busy. Could you just write it for me and I'll sign it? What do you do? That, again, it's a nice situation, but what do you do in that situation? Yes, I've actually been in that situation. I don't like to do that. I like to get some idea of what's going on, but again, it's okay to make this a cooperative effort. People want to sound good in their testimonials. They want to do the job a testimonial is supposed to do. If they're incoherent, if their language skills are not very good, if English is their second language, and you help them get it into a state where it's more coherent, it's easier to read, that kind of editing, that is allowable. That's fine. You're merely helping them communicate. It doesn't have to be a point in time where they said what they said exactly, and all stutters and all of them and all of us need to be in there. You can't clean it up a little bit, but you do need to get a final okay from them if you do this. My advice, if somebody says just do it, interview them first. It can be a couple of minutes. What was your life before? You got my advice or my product or my services, and what happened afterwards? That usually solves it, and people will say, oh, well, before, we weren't making e-sales at all in that department. Now it's the highest performing department in the company. That's your testimonial right there. You help them, but you only help them clarify what was the truth. Yeah, I'd say that's a good example of you would never say, you probably don't want to say this, and I'm going to record you saying it. However, coaching is allowed. Another question we get frequently, is it okay to edit them? If someone goes on, it's a good testimony, but it's too long for this space, can I edit it? It helped me. Can I put the accurate product name in there and use the rest of the quote? Without making you say all again, the answer is yes, you can, as long as they agree. The bottom line is you do want something that this real person will really stand behind. They're not experts in writing testimonials, so coaching them to talk about this or address this is fine and legal and ethical as long as in the end, they're like, yep, yeah, that's a statement I would make, I agree with that statement. Okay, the testimonials you put up, except for all but one, in fact, it kind of gives us a good example. Some people are happy to give a testimonial, but they don't want to give their full name, their photo, their hometown, sometimes although it's strange, they don't use their website. What do you do in that situation? Well, there's a couple of different ways. One was I was doing some work in the self-defense industry, and we had an active Navy SEAL give a testimonial who could not reveal who he was. It was part of his agreement with the SEALs. So that in that instance, I just said signed SEAL Team 6 member, Prentissi who participated in blah, blah, blah exercise, something like that. So that's just another part of the story. And I would say he cannot, you know, Prentissi, he cannot give his actual name even though he wanted to because of military secrets law or something like that. For another person who doesn't want to do it, you'll see a lot of testimonials in a lot of ads where it says Jane Dee, Kansas City, Ohio. Those tend to be the lowest form of testimonials because somebody said something, they're not willing to put their name on it, or in a lot of cases, those are made up. So you're fighting against the tide of unethical marketers out there who are making up testimonials and using them as if they were real. So the more real you can make them, the better off you are. In some instances, you just can't do it. They need to remain anonymous. If you can explain why they need to be anonymous, that helps position that. If you can't, if they just didn't want to do it but the testimony was just so damn good, you wanted to use it, then you take a shot. You say Jane Dee from Kansas City. Or you can tell little stories. A frustrated housewife from Kansas City, Prentissi who refuses to be named because it's just too embarrassing. You can make these little stories. It does subtract a little bit from the credibility, but it keeps the narrative going. So there are ways around that. There's ways around everything, by the way. Well, yeah, I think that's the way I've always encouraged people is, what do you have? If you've been in business 10, 20 years, we can't use all the testimonials. But it wasn't like that day one when you hung your shingle out there. But it's this overtime, if you follow these techniques. Another thing that I recommend to people, even if you don't think you're going to use testimonials and you're advertising, or you want to skip this today, it's a good practice to get into asking for them. Because what I've seen, both for myself and for people I've done some consulting work with, is frequently your customers have a really good way of verbalizing a benefit that you didn't think of. So by not giving them a script or not over prompting them, by letting them be themselves and talk about how this helped them, while generally what John said, you'll want to look for testimonials to back up your benefits, this can actually help you better understand your benefits or help you restructure your ad. So it's a very powerful technique, even if your plan is not currently to use many or any of them in your advertising. All right, as I said, this is one of those areas. As you get into your own specifics, you'll have some questions. If you're in the coaching program, your coach will give you some good feedback. For right now, as John will explain, if you don't have exact testimonials that are real for your product, that's okay. There's still an exercise we'd like you to do that'll help you get better at this, using this technique in your advertising. Okay, now we're going to get into what I call a fantasy session. I want you to write six testimonials, and if you don't have access to actual customers who have offered to give testimonials, or you don't have testimonials that you can already work with, it's okay just for this exercise to just fantasize what the best thing that a prospect could or might say about your product or service, so that you get the idea of how they actually are constructed. I want you to use these, never use up made up testimonials, but I want you to write out a testimonial as the perfect prospect would say it. Also, let's do three of those. Just use benefits that you want to highlight or address objections that people have. Do a before and after one. Just before I encountered your stuff, I was blank and then blank. Just write that out and it'll give you a good idea of how testimonials are constructed. Also write one testimonial for a product that you've actually gotten something from. I actually think about a real situation where you like the product. It would be a car you drove recently, an airline you flew, something you bought at the store. It's not going to be used, but go ahead and write it out. Write out a before and after if you want, or write out a very specific type of testimonial, just something we can work with. Yeah, remember this is just an exercise. We're not going to use these. If you are in a situation where you're in the coaching program and you do have people you can interview and maybe get a testimonial out and you can do it quickly, that would be acceptable. But it's not absolutely necessary because we don't need to use these testimonials. We just need you to get used to writing them out so you understand what goes into a testimonial and how that works. So just a few testimonials and I'll see you on the other side.
